title: The Mystery of the Vanished Ark of the Covenant
description:
The Ark of the Covenant of God is among the most mighty and mysterious vessels within the Holy Scriptures. In the days of Moses, according unto the pattern which God shewed him, it was fashioned of shittim wood, and overlaid with pure gold, both within and without. Upon the mercy seat thereof did two cherubims stretch forth their wings on high. Within this Ark were laid up the tables of stone bearing the ten commandments, Aaron's rod that budded, and the golden pot that had manna. It stood as the very similitude of God's presence and His throne amidst the children of Israel.

[IMG1]

An exceeding divine power pertained unto this Ark. When the priests that bare it dipped their feet in the brim of the water of Jordan, the waters were parted asunder. When they compassed the city of Jericho bearing the Ark, the great walls thereof fell down flat of their own accord. Once, when the Philistines took it and brought it into the house of Dagon their god, behold, the idol fell upon his face to the earth before the Ark, and was broken in pieces. So long as the Ark was in their midst, the children of Israel obtained triumphant victory in battle.

[IMG2]

Yet herein lieth an exceeding great mystery. In the great temple which king Solomon built, the Ark was kept in sure safety. But when Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon destroyed Jerusalem and carried away the vessels of the house of the LORD, the name of the Ark was nowhere written in the register thereof. There is no record in the Scriptures that it fell into the hands of the enemy, nor that they carried it away. From that time forth, what became of the Ark, or where it is hidden, remaineth a profound mystery unto this day. Notwithstanding, it is a marvellous truth that in the Revelation of John 11:19, the apostle saw in a vision that the temple of God was opened in heaven, and there was seen in His temple the Ark of His testament.

[IMG3]

title: The Mystery of Melchizedek - The Priest Without Beginning or End
description:
A most mysterious figure who appeareth suddenly in the book of Genesis, and passeth out of sight, is 'Melchizedek'. When Abraham returned from the slaughter of the kings, having delivered his kinsman Lot from the hands of the enemy, Melchizedek king of Salem (which is Jerusalem) came forth to meet him. He was the priest of the most high God. And he brought forth bread and wine, and blessed Abraham. Theron, Abraham gave him tithes of all the spoils.

[IMG1]

By interpretation, the name Melchizedek signifieth 'King of righteousness', and after that also King of Salem, which is, 'King of peace'. In the seventh chapter of the Epistle to the Hebrews in the New Testament, a great mystery concerning him is revealed. This Melchizedek is without father, without mother, without descent. He hath neither beginning of days, nor end of life; meaning he is without birth or death. The Scripture plainly declareth that he is made like unto the Son of God, and abideth a priest continually.

[IMG2]

For a man to be without father, mother, birth, or death is a mystery that passeth human understanding. Therefore, many scholars of the Word do believe that this was the Lord Jesus Christ Himself, appearing unto Abraham in the form of Melchizedek, in a theophany before His incarnation. The bread and wine which he brought forth are a figure of the Lord's supper. Furthermore, the Lord sware in Psalm 110:4 concerning the coming Messiah, saying, "Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchizedek," thereby strengthening this marvellous mystery.

[IMG3]

title: The Mystery of the Nephilim - The Sons of God and the Daughters of Men
description:
In the sixth chapter of Genesis is recorded a profound mystery that came to pass before the flood. When men began to multiply on the face of the earth, the "sons of God" saw the daughters of men that they were fair; and they took them wives of all which they chose. From this union were born upon the earth giants, known as the "Nephilim." The Scripture describeth them as mighty men which were of old, men of renown.

[IMG1]

Who these 'sons of God' truly were remaineth a matter of great disputation among the learned to this day. Some say they were fallen angels, which kept not their first estate, and took unto themselves the daughters of men, as it is grounded upon the epistle of Jude 1:6. Others believe they were the godly descendants of Seth, who mingled themselves with the ungodly daughters of the line of Cain. Howsoever it be, their generation brought forth grievous wickedness and violence upon the earth.

[IMG2]

The stature and might of these Nephilim were manifold greater than those of common men. The great wickedness which increased by reason of them was a chief cause wherefore God destroyed the world of that time by the flood. Yet even after the flood, in Numbers 13:33, when the children of Israel went to spy out the land of Canaan, they saw the giants, the sons of Anak, which come of the giants (Nephilim): and they were in their own sight as grasshoppers, and so they were in the sight of the giants. This remaineth one of the most compelling mysteries in the Holy Writ.

[IMG3]

title: The Mystery of Enoch's Translation - The Man Who Tasted Not Death
description:
Enoch is the first of two men in the Scriptures who tasted not death, being translated directly into heaven by God. In the fifth chapter of Genesis, in the book of the generations of Adam, after declaring the days of every man, it is written, "and he died." But concerning Enoch, a most marvellous word is recorded: "And Enoch walked with God: and he was not; for God took him" (Genesis 5:24).

[IMG1]

To walk with God meaneth that Enoch yielded full obedience unto Him, and held a most intimate communion with the Almighty. In the Epistle to the Hebrews 11:5, this mystery is expounded more plainly: "By faith Enoch was translated that he should not see death; and was not found, because God had translated him: for before his translation he had this testimony, that he pleased God." For a mortal man so perfectly to please God is a great wonder in the annals of human history.

[IMG2]

What truly came to pass upon that day? What became of his fleshly tabernacle? How did God take him? These things are nowhere expounded in the Scriptures. It was the highest reward bestowed upon him by God for shewing an undefiled devotion in the midst of a corrupted generation, even before the flood. That he also prophesied of the Lord's coming, as it is written in Jude 1:14, doth testify to the depth of his spiritual measure, and further multiplieth the mystery concerning him in the Holy Bible.

[IMG3]

title: The Mystery of the Witch of Endor - The Apparition of Samuel's Spirit
description:
This is a dreadful and dark event which came to pass in the twilight of the life of Saul, the first king of Israel. In the eight and twentieth chapter of the first book of Samuel, when the Philistines gathered themselves together to war against Israel, Saul was sore afraid. When he enquired of the LORD, the LORD answered him not, neither by dreams, nor by prophets. It was a time when the Spirit of the LORD had departed from Saul. Being in great distress, Saul disguised himself, and went unto a woman that had a familiar spirit at Endor.

[IMG1]

Saul besought her, and desired that she should bring up the spirit of Samuel the prophet, who was dead. When the woman practiced her arts, and saw an old man coming up out of the earth, she cried with a loud voice for fear. And Saul perceived that it was Samuel, and he stooped with his face to the ground, and bowed himself. Then the spirit rebuked Saul, saying, "Why hast thou disquieted me, to bring me up?" Furthermore, he pronounced a sure judgment, that on the morrow Saul and his sons should die in the battle.

[IMG2]

Herein a great question doth confront the scholars. Can a witch in truth bring up the spirit of God's holy prophet from the realm of the dead? Certain learned men do believe it was a demonic spirit masquerading in the likeness of Samuel. Yet, seeing that the things spoken by the spirit came to pass exactly on the morrow, other scholars do earnestly contend, resting upon the words of the Scripture, that God Himself permitted the spirit of Samuel to appear for to pronounce judgment upon Saul. This remaineth one of the most fearful mysteries of the Bible.

[IMG3]

title: The Mystery of the Urim and Thummim - The Stones of Divine Counsel
description:
In the days of the Old Testament, the most mysterious elements used by the high priest to enquire of the will of God were the 'Urim and Thummim'. In Exodus 28:30, God commanded that they be put in the breastplate of judgment, that they might be upon Aaron's heart when he went in before the LORD. By interpretation from the Hebrew tongue, these words do signify "Lights and Perfections."

[IMG1]

Whensoever the children of Israel were in sore straits, and most chiefly when they took counsel whether they should go out to battle or no, the high priest enquired of the LORD by them. For an ensample, in 1 Samuel 28:6, when Saul enquired of the LORD, it is written that "the LORD answered him not, neither by dreams, nor by Urim, nor by prophets." But how did they work? Were they stones, or were they precious jewels? By what manner did God shew His answer of "Yea" or "Nay" through them? This remaineth a mystery unto this day.

[IMG2]

According unto the belief of some wise men, these stones would shine forth with a marvellous light according to the answer of God. Certain ancient Jewish writings do declare that God shewed His message by the illuminating of the letters in the names of the twelve tribes engraven upon the breastplate. But the true fashion of them, and the manner of their working, are nowhere expounded in the Scriptures. After the Babylonian captivity (in the year 586 B.C.), they vanished away forever. That wondrous manner whereby men communed with God is left as a heavenly mystery.

[IMG3]

title: The Mystery of the Star of Bethlehem That Guided the Wise Men
description:
The 'Star of Bethlehem', which guided the wise men from the east at the time of the nativity of Jesus Christ, is among the most wondrous celestial mysteries in the Bible. According to the second chapter of the Gospel of Matthew, these wise men saw a peculiar star in the east, and perceiving that the King of the Jews was born, they came to Jerusalem. The Scripture declareth that the star went before them, guiding their way, till it came and stood over where the young child was.

[IMG1]

Yet what in truth was this star? How could an ordinary star move across the firmament and stand still, pointing exactly unto a lowly house? Over hundreds of years, astronomers and scholars of the Word have searched into this matter. Some do reckon it to have been a rare conjunction of the planets Jupiter and Saturn. Others have reasoned that it was a comet, or the bursting forth of a supernova.

[IMG2]

But judging by the description in the Holy Writ, it was no mere natural phenomenon of the heavens. Scholars of the Word do earnestly believe that it was a divine light, even the Shekinah glory, which God Himself brought forth to declare the birth of His Son unto the world, or peradventure a host of angels guiding the way in the likeness of a star. For neither planets nor comets can point precisely unto a specific house upon the earth. This Star of Bethlehem remaineth a wondrous mystery of God, which passeth the understanding of science even unto this day.

[IMG3]
